content.text: Shared auth and telemetry libraries are centrally maintained, internally published software packages that implement platform-wide authentication, authorization, and observability instrumentation in a reusable, versioned form. Rather than requiring each agent service team to implement JWT validation, service identity, OpenTelemetry tracing, structured logging, and standard metrics collection independently, the platform team builds these once and publishes them to an internal package registry. Service teams add the libraries as dependencies and call their APIs; the complex implementation details of token validation, context propagation, span serialization, and log formatting are handled by the library.

These libraries are distinct from the auto-instrumentation provided by OTel agents or service meshes: they expose application-level APIs that developers call explicitly to instrument business logic, record LLM-specific metrics (token counts, model selection, per-call cost), validate incoming JWTs with service-specific claim requirements, and propagate trace context through async task queues and multi-agent call chains. They complement infrastructure-level instrumentation rather than replacing it.

Consistent auth and telemetry across the fleet is only achievable if the implementation is shared rather than reimplemented independently by each team. When each team implements JWT validation separately, subtle differences in claim validation logic, token expiration handling, and error responses accumulate over time, creating inconsistencies that are discovered during security audits or incident investigations. When each team implements metrics collection separately, metric names, label schemas, and aggregation choices differ, making fleet-wide dashboards impossible.

Shared libraries also accelerate remediation of security vulnerabilities. When a CVE is discovered in a JWT validation library, the platform team updates the shared auth library, releases a new version, and opens automated upgrade PRs for every service that depends on it. Compared to the alternative — identifying which of the dozens of independent implementations uses the vulnerable library and coordinating upgrades with each team — this represents a significant reduction in the time to remediate a critical vulnerability.
Security fixes in auth implementations propagate to all services via a single library release.
Consistent metric names and label schemas enable fleet-wide dashboards without per-service configuration.
Teams consume a tested, documented API rather than implementing complex protocols from scratch.
Library versioning provides a migration path when auth or telemetry protocols change.
The platform team can add new observability capabilities (cost tracking, safety filtering events) to all services by releasing a new library version.
Centralized testing of auth and telemetry code is more thorough than per-team testing of individual implementations.
A bug in the shared auth library can affect all services simultaneously — high blast radius for library-level defects.
Library APIs become implicit contracts; changing them requires coordinated migration across all consumers.
Teams may be blocked on platform team bandwidth when they need auth or telemetry customizations the library does not yet support.
Internal package registries require maintenance infrastructure (registry hosting, authentication, index updates).
Python and JavaScript ecosystems require separate libraries for the same platform functionality, doubling maintenance work.

The platform team maintains library repositories in the internal monorepo or dedicated repos, publishes releases to an internal PyPI or npm registry, and communicates releases through a library release channel. Libraries use semantic versioning: patch releases for bug fixes, minor releases for backward-compatible additions, major releases for breaking API changes. Each release includes a changelog entry, and major releases include a migration guide and a compatibility shim that allows gradual migration.

Service teams add the library as a versioned dependency in their `pyproject.toml` or `package.json` and call the library's initialization API during service startup. The auth library's initialization registers JWT validation middleware with the service's HTTP framework; the telemetry library's initialization sets up the OTel tracer provider, metrics registry, and log processor with the service name and environment from environment variables. After initialization, instrumentation happens through the library's decorators and context managers.

Renovate bot or Dependabot monitors the library versions pinned in each service's dependency manifest. When the platform team releases a new library version, Renovate opens upgrade PRs for all services within 24 hours. Teams review and merge; services that do not merge within the SLA receive escalation. For major library versions with migration guides, the upgrade PR includes a link to the migration guide and a summary of the required changes.

attrs.alt: Shared auth and telemetry library architecture

content.text: Extract auth and telemetry into shared libraries when two or more teams have independently implemented the same patterns and the implementations have diverged in ways that create operational inconsistency. Earlier extraction, before divergence occurs, is better: the starter template approach means that teams adopt the shared library at project generation time rather than building their own first.

Separate the auth library from the telemetry library rather than bundling them into a single platform SDK. Auth and telemetry have different change rates — auth changes when security requirements evolve, telemetry changes when observability standards evolve — and different teams may have legitimate reasons to adopt a new auth library version without simultaneously adopting a new telemetry library version. Independent versioning of independent concerns reduces mandatory coupling.

# platform_auth/__init__.py — shared auth library public API
from typing import Optional, List
from functools import wraps
import httpx, jwt, time
from cachetools import TTLCache

_JWKS_CACHE: Optional[dict] = None
_TOKEN_CACHE: TTLCache = TTLCache(maxsize=1024, ttl=300)

def init_auth(jwks_uri: str, audience: str, issuer: str):
    """Call once at service startup."""
    global _jwks_uri, _audience, _issuer
    _jwks_uri = jwks_uri
    _audience = audience
    _issuer = issuer

def validate_jwt(token: str) -> dict:
    """Validate JWT and return claims. Raises AuthError on failure."""
    if token in _TOKEN_CACHE:
        return _TOKEN_CACHE[token]
    jwks = _get_jwks()
    try:
        claims = jwt.decode(
            token, jwks,
            algorithms=["RS256"],
            audience=_audience,
            issuer=_issuer,
        )
        _TOKEN_CACHE[token] = claims
        return claims
    except jwt.ExpiredSignatureError:
        raise AuthError("Token expired")
    except jwt.InvalidClaimError as e:
        raise AuthError(f"Invalid claim: {e}")

def require_roles(*roles: str):
    """Decorator that validates JWT and checks for required roles."""
    def decorator(func):
        @wraps(func)
        async def wrapper(request, *args, **kwargs):
            auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization", "")
            if not auth_header.startswith("Bearer "):
                raise AuthError("Missing Bearer token")
            claims = validate_jwt(auth_header[7:])
            user_roles = claims.get("roles", [])
            if not any(r in user_roles for r in roles):
                raise AuthError(f"Required role not present: {roles}")
            request.state.claims = claims
            return await func(request, *args, **kwargs)
        return wrapper
    return decorator

class AuthError(Exception):
    pass

# platform_telemetry/__init__.py — shared telemetry library public API
from opentelemetry import trace, metrics
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.metric_exporter import OTLPMetricExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PeriodicExportingMetricReader
import structlog, os

_tracer: trace.Tracer = None
_meter: metrics.Meter = None

def init_telemetry(service_name: str, environment: str):
    """Call once at service startup. Reads OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT from env."""
    global _tracer, _meter
    endpoint = os.environ["OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT"]

    tp = TracerProvider()
    tp.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint=endpoint)))
    trace.set_tracer_provider(tp)
    _tracer = trace.get_tracer(service_name)

    mp = MeterProvider(readers=[PeriodicExportingMetricReader(
        OTLPMetricExporter(endpoint=endpoint), export_interval_millis=30_000
    )])
    metrics.set_meter_provider(mp)
    _meter = metrics.get_meter(service_name)

    structlog.configure(processors=[
        structlog.contextvars.merge_contextvars,
        structlog.stdlib.add_log_level,
        structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt="iso"),
        structlog.processors.JSONRenderer(),
    ])

    return _tracer, _meter

def get_tracer() -> trace.Tracer:
    return _tracer

def get_meter() -> metrics.Meter:
    return _meter

def record_llm_call(provider: str, model: str, prompt_tokens: int,
                    completion_tokens: int, cost_usd: float):
    """Record standard LLM usage metrics. Call after every LLM API response."""
    _meter.create_counter("agent_llm_tokens_total").add(
        prompt_tokens, {"provider": provider, "model": model, "type": "prompt"})
    _meter.create_counter("agent_llm_tokens_total").add(
        completion_tokens, {"provider": provider, "model": model, "type": "completion"})
    _meter.create_counter("agent_llm_cost_usd_total").add(
        cost_usd, {"provider": provider, "model": model})

A new agent service team uses the auth and telemetry libraries from the starter template. During local development, they initialize the auth library with a local JWKS endpoint and call `require_roles('agent:write')` on their mutation endpoints. During testing, they pass a test JWT generated with their service's audience claim; the library validates it without network calls due to TTL caching. In production, the JWKS URI points to the platform identity provider, and the library fetches and caches public keys automatically.

The platform team discovers that a JWT validation library used by the shared auth library has a critical CVE allowing certain malformed tokens to bypass expiration validation. They patch the shared auth library, release version 2.3.1, and Renovate opens upgrade PRs for all 47 services that depend on `platform-auth`. Within 48 hours, 43 services have merged the upgrade PR; the remaining 4 are in a code freeze and have acknowledged the finding. The platform team closes the vulnerability within 72 hours of disclosure — a timeline that would have been impossible if each team maintained its own auth implementation.

A team adding a new capability — multi-step reasoning traces that need to correlate spans across multiple LLM calls within a single user request — discovers that the telemetry library does not yet support setting custom span names for tool invocations. They open a feature request, implement the capability in the shared library with platform team review, and release it as a minor version. All services that upgrade receive the capability; the implementing team's service was the first to use it.

Stripe's internal engineering blog describes their approach to shared platform libraries: a small platform team maintains core libraries for authentication (JWT validation against their internal identity provider), metrics (Prometheus client initialization with standard label schemas), and tracing (OTel SDK initialization with sampling configuration). These libraries are distributed through their internal package registry, versioned with semantic versioning, and monitored by their dependency bot which opens upgrade PRs when new versions are released. The approach allows Stripe to maintain consistent security and observability properties across hundreds of services without requiring each team to be a security or observability expert.
Publishing a library that requires complex initialization configuration, so teams configure it incorrectly and get silent failures rather than helpful errors.
Releasing breaking API changes in minor versions, surprising teams who upgrade expecting backward compatibility.
Building auth and telemetry into a single large SDK, making it impossible to upgrade one without upgrading the other.
Not testing the library against the full range of framework versions used by consuming services, causing compatibility failures on upgrade.
Providing no migration path when a major version has breaking changes, forcing teams to spend significant time on upgrades.
Allowing library issues to block consuming teams for days due to slow platform team response time; triage of library issues should be fast.
Design library initialization APIs that fail loudly with descriptive errors when required configuration is missing or invalid.
Maintain backward-compatible APIs for at least two minor version cycles before marking them as removed in a major version.
Publish a machine-readable compatibility matrix that documents which library versions work with which versions of the major frameworks (FastAPI, Flask, etc.).
Include integration tests in the library repository that run against a containerized platform stack to catch regressions before release.
Provide example usage in the library repository for the most common patterns: FastAPI middleware, async task queues, multi-agent call chains.
Establish and publish SLOs for library issue response time so teams know what to expect when they file bugs.

JWT validation with public key caching adds minimal overhead to request processing — typically under 1ms after the first request per key rotation cycle. Uncached validation requiring a JWKS endpoint fetch can take 50-200ms; the TTL cache in the auth library ensures that JWKS fetches happen at most once per cache TTL (typically 5-15 minutes) rather than per request. Monitor the `auth_jwks_fetch_duration` metric to detect unexpected increases in JWKS fetch latency that might indicate problems with the identity provider.

OpenTelemetry span creation and batch export have measurable overhead at very high request rates. The shared telemetry library should configure tail-based sampling with the OTel collector so that not every span is exported: configure the collector to sample 100% of error and slow spans and a fraction of normal spans. The service itself does not need to implement sampling logic — it creates all spans, and the collector makes sampling decisions downstream. This simplifies library design while keeping export volume manageable.

The auth library is a security-critical component: a vulnerability in it affects every service that uses it. The library repository must require code review from at least one security-aware platform team member for every change, regardless of change size. Automated security scanning (SAST, dependency vulnerability scanning) must run on every PR. Release tags must be signed so that consuming services can verify library integrity before installation.

Reliability of the telemetry library is important but not security-critical in the same way. The library should be designed so that telemetry failures are non-fatal: if the OTel collector is unreachable, the service continues processing requests and logs the telemetry failure rather than raising an exception. Include circuit breakers and retry budgets in the exporter configuration. Telemetry library failures should be observable — expose a metric that counts telemetry export failures so the platform team can detect and investigate pipeline issues.
Shared auth and telemetry libraries consolidate security-critical and observability-critical code in one place, enabling consistent implementation and fast vulnerability remediation.
Publish to an internal package registry with semantic versioning and automated upgrade PRs via Renovate or Dependabot.
Separate auth and telemetry into independent libraries with independent versioning to avoid mandatory coupling.
Design initialization APIs that fail loudly; silent misconfiguration is harder to debug than an explicit startup error.
TTL-cached JWT validation and OTel batch export are the two critical performance properties the library must implement correctly.
Auth library changes require security-aware code review; telemetry library failures must be non-fatal to service operation.
